name: Redis OM Python (Redis Object Mapping)

auth.notes: Redis AUTH password or TLS client certs. REDIS_OM_URL env var.

performance.notes: Redis in-memory performance; ORM overhead minimal

agent_readiness.notes: Pydantic-based ORM for Redis. from redis_om import HashModel, Field, Migrator. class Customer(HashModel): first_name: str. last_name: str. email: str = Field(index=True). age: int. Customer.create(first_name='John', last_name='Doe', email='j@d.com', age=30). Customer.find(Customer.email == 'j@d.com').all(). Customer.get(pk). HashModel stores as Redis hash. JsonModel stores as JSON (requires RedisJSON module). Requires: RediSearch for queries, RedisJSON for JsonModel. Migrator().run() creates indexes. Async: AsyncHashModel. REDIS_OM_URL=redis://localhost:6379.

gotchas: Full query support requires RediSearch module (Redis Stack or Redis Cloud)
Not suitable for complex relational data — it's Redis, not a relational DB
PKs are ULIDs by default — opaque but sortable
Migrator().run() must be called to create search indexes
best_when: Storing and querying Python objects in Redis with Pydantic schemas
Session storage, leaderboards, real-time data with search
FastAPI + Redis Stack for fast object storage with search
When Redis is already in the stack and you want ORM ergonomics
avoid_when: Standard Redis without RediSearch module (limited query support)
Persistent primary data storage (Redis is in-memory)
summary: Redis OM Python maps Pydantic models to Redis. class Customer(HashModel): email: str = Field(index=True); Customer.create(email='x@y.com'); Customer.find(Customer.email == 'x@y.com').all(). Requires RediSearch for queries. JsonModel needs RedisJSON. MIT by Redis Inc. Migrator().run() for indexes. Best for FastAPI+Redis Stack. Not for relational data.
